defaultResponse: I don't have a specific answer for that yet. Try asking about Angular components, signals, services, routing, directives, RxJS, forms, or HTTP. You can also browse the Learn section for detailed lessons!
suggestedQuestions: What is the difference between @Input and input()?
How do I make HTTP requests?

responses.question: What is Angular?
responses.answer:
Angular is a TypeScript-based, open-source web framework built and maintained by Google. It is used to build Single Page Applications (SPAs) — web apps that load once and dynamically update without full page reloads.

Angular provides a complete, opinionated solution out of the box: routing, forms, HTTP client, animations, dependency injection, and testing tools — all included.

responses.question: What is a Component?
responses.answer:
A Component is the fundamental building block of an Angular application. Every visible part of the UI is a component.

A component consists of:
• TypeScript class — the logic and data
• HTML template — the view
• Optional CSS/SCSS styles

It is decorated with @Component which configures the selector (custom HTML tag), template, and styles.

responses.question: What are Angular Signals?
responses.answer:
Signals are Angular's new reactive primitive (introduced in Angular 16, stable in Angular 17+). A Signal is a wrapper around a value that notifies consumers when the value changes.

Three core functions:
• signal(value) — creates a writable signal
• computed(() => ...) — creates a read-only derived signal
• effect(() => ...) — runs side-effects when signals change

Signals replace Zone.js for change detection, making Angular apps faster and more predictable.

responses.question: What is Dependency Injection?
responses.answer:
Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern where a class receives its dependencies from external sources rather than creating them itself. Angular has a built-in DI system.

You declare a service with @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) and Angular creates a single shared instance (singleton) of it across the entire app.

Modern Angular uses the inject() function instead of constructor injection.

responses.question: How does Angular Routing work?
responses.answer:
Angular Router is a client-side routing library that maps URL paths to components without reloading the page.

Key concepts:
• Routes array — maps paths to components
• <router-outlet> — placeholder where the matched component renders
• routerLink — directive for navigation links
• loadComponent() — lazy loads components (better performance)
• Guards — protect routes (e.g., authentication)

responses.question: What are the types of Data Binding?
responses.answer:
Angular has 4 types of data binding:

① Interpolation {{ }} — displays component data in the template
② Property Binding [prop] — binds component data to an HTML property
③ Event Binding (event) — listens to DOM events and calls a method
④ Two-way Binding [(ngModel)] — syncs template ↔ component (requires FormsModule)

responses.question: What are Directives?
responses.answer:
Directives extend HTML with custom behavior. Angular has 3 types:

① Component directives — components ARE directives with a template
② Structural directives — change the DOM structure (@if, @for, @switch)
③ Attribute directives — change appearance/behavior of an element

Angular 17+ uses the new control flow syntax (@if, @for) instead of *ngIf and *ngFor.
responses.codeExample:
@if (isLoggedIn) {

Welcome back!

} @else {

Please log in.

} @for (item of items; track item.id) {
  • {{ item.name }}
    • } @empty {
  • No items found.
    • }

    Toggle class

responses.question: What are Lifecycle Hooks?
responses.answer:
Lifecycle hooks are methods that Angular calls at specific stages of a component's life:

• ngOnInit() — runs once after component is initialized (best for data loading)
• ngOnChanges() — runs when input properties change
• ngOnDestroy() — runs just before the component is destroyed (cleanup subscriptions)
• ngAfterViewInit() — runs after the component's view is rendered
• ngDoCheck() — runs on every change detection cycle

With Signals, many use-cases for lifecycle hooks are handled by effect() instead.

responses.question: What is the difference between @Input and input()?
What is RxJS?
responses.answer:
RxJS (Reactive Extensions for JavaScript) is a library for reactive programming using Observables. Angular uses it extensively for handling async operations like HTTP calls, events, and routing.

Key concepts:
• Observable — a stream of values over time
• Observer — subscribes to an Observable
• Operators — transform streams (map, filter, switchMap, mergeMap, etc.)
• Subject — both Observable and Observer; used for multicasting
• async pipe — auto-subscribes in templates (auto-cleanup)

responses.question: How do I make HTTP requests in Angular?
responses.answer:
Angular provides HttpClient for making HTTP requests. It returns Observables that you subscribe to.

Setup: Add provideHttpClient() in app.config.ts
Usage: inject HttpClient and call .get(), .post(), .put(), .delete()

responses.question: What is a Standalone Component?
responses.answer:
Standalone components (default in Angular 17+) don't belong to an NgModule. They declare their own dependencies directly in the imports[] array of @Component.

Benefits:
• Simpler — no need to create or manage NgModules
• More portable — self-contained and reusable
• Better tree-shaking — only imports what it needs
• Easier lazy loading — just use loadComponent()

responses.question: What are Reactive Forms?
responses.answer:
Reactive Forms are the programmatic, model-driven approach to forms in Angular. You define the form structure in the component class using FormGroup and FormControl.

Advantages over Template-driven forms:
• Full control in the component (testable)
• Dynamic form fields
• Complex validation logic
• Better for large forms

responses.question: How does Angular Change Detection work?
responses.answer:
Change Detection is Angular's mechanism for syncing the component's state with the DOM.

Default strategy: Angular checks ALL components on every event.
OnPush strategy: Angular only checks a component if its @Input() references change, an event fires from the component, or an Observable emits.

With Signals (Angular 17+): Angular is moving toward signal-based, zoneless change detection — components only re-render when a signal they read changes. Much more efficient!

responses.question: What are Pipes in Angular?
responses.answer:
Pipes transform data for display in templates. Use them with the | operator.

Built-in pipes: date, uppercase, lowercase, currency, percent, async, json, slice, keyvalue

async pipe: Automatically subscribes to an Observable or Promise and unsubscribes when the component is destroyed — very useful!

responses.question: What is a Service and when should I use it?
responses.answer:
A Service is a TypeScript class decorated with @Injectable that holds:
• Shared business logic (calculations, transformations)
• Shared state (user session, theme, cart)
• API calls (HTTP requests)
• Utilities used across multiple components

Rule of thumb: If more than one component needs it, put it in a service.

responses.question: What is ViewChild?
responses.answer:
ViewChild gives you direct access to a child component or DOM element from the parent component class.

Modern Angular 17+ uses viewChild() signal function instead of the @ViewChild() decorator.

responses.question: What are Route Guards?
responses.answer:
Route Guards are functions that decide whether navigation to a route should be allowed. They are used for authentication, authorization, or preventing navigation when there are unsaved changes.

Modern Angular uses functional guards (plain functions) instead of class-based guards.

responses.question: What is the new Angular template syntax?
responses.answer: Angular 17+ introduced a new built-in control flow syntax using @if, @for, @switch — replacing *ngIf, *ngFor, and *ngSwitch directives. The new syntax is faster (no directive overhead) and cleaner to read.
responses.codeExample:
@if (user) {

Welcome, {{ user.name }}

} @else if (loading) {

Loading...

} @else { Sign in } @for (item of items; track item.id) {
  • {{ item.name }}
    • } @empty {

    No items.

    } @switch (status) { @case ('active') { Active } @case ('inactive') { Inactive } @default { Unknown } }

responses.question: What is Lazy Loading?
responses.answer:
Lazy Loading means a component or module is only downloaded when the user navigates to it, instead of loading everything upfront. This dramatically reduces the initial bundle size and speeds up the first load.

In Angular, you use loadComponent() for standalone components or loadChildren() for routes arrays.

responses.question: What are Angular best practices?
responses.answer:
Key Angular best practices:

① Use standalone components (no NgModules)
② Use signal-based state (signal, computed, effect)
③ Use inject() instead of constructor injection
④ Lazy load all feature routes with loadComponent()
⑤ Use OnPush change detection for performance
⑥ Keep components small — extract logic into services
⑦ Use the async pipe in templates to avoid manual subscriptions
⑧ Feature-based folder structure (not type-based)
⑨ Use TypeScript strict mode
⑩ Write unit tests for services and components
responses.codeExample:
// ✅ Feature-based folder structure
src/app/
├── core/              // singleton services, guards, interceptors
│   ├── services/
│   └── guards/
├── features/          // one folder per page/feature
│   ├── home/
│   ├── dashboard/
│   └── profile/
└── shared/            // reusable components, pipes, directives
    ├── components/
    ├── pipes/
    └── models/
